snapshots.name: Anna Finch

otherLoans.note: Repayments begin above £27,295 threshold — not yet earning enough to repay

alerts.headline: No emergency fund — 1 payslip from crisis
alerts.context: £2,400 cash covers less than 1 month of essential expenses at £28,000/yr income.
alerts.action: Build emergency fund to 3 months first

alerts.headline: Auto-enrolment only — no named beneficiary
alerts.context: Your Nest pension has no nomination. If you die, the trustees decide who receives it.
alerts.action: Name a beneficiary on your pension

alerts.headline: £1,800 ISA — maximise before tax year end
alerts.context: You have £18,200 remaining ISA allowance this year. Shelter savings from future tax.

aiSuggestions: How much should I save each month to build a 3-month emergency fund by the end of this year?
Should I pay into my pension or build an ISA first?
Does my student loan affect my ability to get a mortgage later?
What happens to my pension if I die — and how do I name a beneficiary?

behaviour.risk: Just starting and already engaged. At 22, showing up is the habit.
behaviour.action: Set up a small automated monthly savings transfer. Any amount. It's the habit that counts.
behaviour.future: Behaviours locked in at 22 compound for 45+ years — nothing else in your life has that leverage.

capital.risk: £7k NW vs a £560k retirement number — exactly where you should be at 22.
capital.action: Your auto-enrolment pension is working. Consider opening an ISA with £25/month.
capital.future: Starting early beats saving more later. A £50/month habit at 22 beats £200/month at 32.

tax.risk: Basic-rate taxpayer. Auto-enrolment pension is the easy win. ISA allowance almost entirely unused.
tax.action: Open an ISA. Any contribution counts — even £10/month uses a wrapper you'll benefit from forever.
tax.future: Early ISA contributions sit in a wrapper that never pays CGT or dividend tax — for life.

protection.risk: No dependants, no mortgage — protection isn't needed yet.
protection.action: Revisit when you take on a mortgage or have dependants.
protection.future: Protection becomes essential when someone else depends on your income. Not now.

cashflow.risk: £2k cash gives you about 1 month runway. The priority below 6 months is cash, not investments.
cashflow.action: Build a 3-month emergency buffer before increasing pension or ISA contributions.
cashflow.future: A cash buffer is what lets you keep invested through dips without being forced to sell.

debt.risk: Debt-free. Keep it that way through your 20s.
debt.action: Avoid consumer debt — credit cards paid in full, no buy-now-pay-later.
debt.future: Consumer debt is the thief of your 30s. Avoiding it in your 20s is the biggest compounding decision you have.

estate.risk: No will needed yet at 22 with minimal assets and no dependants.
estate.action: Revisit if you buy property or have a long-term partner.
estate.future: Estate planning starts when there's something to leave and someone to leave it to.

snapshots.name: Anna Finch

alerts.headline: No guardian named — new baby
alerts.context: Your will is outdated and names no guardian for your 1-year-old. If both parents die, the courts decide.
alerts.action: Update will and name a guardian

alerts.headline: Life insurance not in trust — adds to estate
alerts.context: £280k life policy sits in your estate and triggers IHT. A trust removes it at no cost.

alerts.headline: No income protection — mortgage at risk
alerts.context: No IP in place. If unable to work for 6+ months, the £1,380/month mortgage payment is at risk.

alerts.headline: Consolidate old pension — Nest pot
alerts.context: £10k Nest pot from previous employer has higher charges and no beneficiary named.

aiSuggestions: How much life insurance do I need with a mortgage, a partner, and a 1-year-old?
Should I focus on the mortgage, pension, or ISA first?
What happens to my child if I die without updating my will?
How does income protection work and what would it cost for my income level?

behaviour.risk: Habits holding up. Mortgage serviced, pension contributions ongoing, ISA used.
behaviour.action: Review direct debits and pension contribution rate after any pay rise.
behaviour.future: Consistent contributions through stage 2 are what fill the gap to your retirement number.

capital.risk: £38k SIPP + £22k ISA + £190k property. Mortgage £295k — NW is temporarily negative, normal at this stage.
capital.action: Focus on pension contribution rate, not NW on paper. Equity builds while you sleep.
capital.future: Stage 2 NW looks weak because leverage dominates — the flipside is the biggest growth phase of your life.

tax.risk: Basic-rate taxpayer. Mortgage payments first, then pension + ISA.
tax.action: Split new savings 60/40 between pension (20% relief) and ISA (flexibility).
tax.future: Staying in the basic-rate band means the pension/ISA split is closer to a toss-up than later when you hit higher-rate.

protection.risk: Life cover in place — good with a mortgage. Critical illness is the missing layer.
protection.action: Add critical illness cover. It's cheaper at 32 than it will ever be.
protection.future: CI cover bought at 32 locks in the rate for decades. Buying at 45 costs significantly more.

cashflow.risk: £18k cash against £295k mortgage — adequate but thin. Build to 6 months of mortgage + bills.
cashflow.action: Hold cash at 6 months of fixed expenses before increasing pension contributions.
cashflow.future: A cash buffer means a job loss or illness doesn't force you to stop pension contributions.

debt.risk: Mortgage is the only debt. Priced in, predictable. No consumer debt is the baseline.
debt.action: Don't accelerate mortgage overpayment — pension contributions at 20% relief beat paying down a ~5% mortgage.
debt.future: Your mortgage will shrink as inflation erodes it. Your pension will compound. Don't mix them.

estate.risk: Likely no will yet. Any assets + partner/children = make one.
estate.action: £100-£300 for an online will, or £500-£1000 for a solicitor-drafted one. Either beats nothing.
estate.future: A will drafted at 32 will serve you fine until your situation meaningfully changes.

snapshots.name: Anna Finch

alerts.headline: No health & welfare LPA — update before children leave school
alerts.context: Financial LPA in place but no health LPA. At 45 with children this is a gap.

alerts.headline: Higher-rate taxpayer — pension contributions maximised?
alerts.context: 40% tax relief on pension contributions. Review salary sacrifice arrangement.

alerts.headline: £20,000 ISA allowance — £88k built, keep growing
alerts.context: Excellent ISA discipline. Consider increasing contributions this year.

aiSuggestions: Am I on track for my retirement income target at 65?
How does salary sacrifice compare to a direct pension contribution for my situation?
What would happen to my mortgage if I couldn't work for 6 months?
Should I be making any IHT planning moves at this stage?

behaviour.risk: Peak engagement stage. Nominations current, both protection layers active, ISA disciplined.
behaviour.action: Book an annual review with a qualified adviser if you haven't. Stage 3 is when advice starts to pay.
behaviour.future: The habits you've built are now compounding visibly — this is where the curve bends.

capital.risk: £210k SIPP + £88k ISA + £260k property. Against a £1.5m retirement target — stage 3 should feel like closing the gap.
capital.action: Model whether to accelerate pension contributions now you're higher-rate.
capital.future: The 40% relief window opens at stage 3 — this is when pension contributions give the biggest lift.

tax.risk: Higher-rate taxpayer. Pension contributions at 40% relief are your biggest tax lever.
tax.action: Maximise pension contributions up to your £60k annual allowance. Use salary sacrifice if your employer offers it.
tax.future: Every £1,000 pension contribution as a higher-rate payer costs you £600 — a 40% head start on compound growth.

protection.risk: Life and critical illness in place — strong at peak-earning stage with dependants at home.
protection.action: Review sum assured against your mortgage balance and earnings — cover should scale as income rises.
protection.future: Protection at 45 protects not just your family but the compound growth of everything you've built.

cashflow.risk: £32k cash against mortgage and family expenses — comfortable, not generous.
cashflow.action: Maintain 6 months of fixed expenses in cash. Higher earners with higher fixed costs need the same ratio.
cashflow.future: A cash buffer becomes more valuable as your earnings rise — replacing a higher income takes longer.

debt.risk: £185k mortgage remaining at stage 3 — on track to be paid by 55-60.
debt.action: Don't pay down mortgage early at the expense of pension 40% relief.
debt.future: Finishing the mortgage naturally around stage 4 leaves stage 5 pension contributions untouched.

estate.risk: With dependent children, a current will and LPA are essential.
estate.action: Review will if dependants have changed. Set up an LPA if you haven't.
estate.future: An LPA at 45 is a small admin cost that protects the next 40 years of financial decisions if anything goes wrong.

snapshots.name: Anna Finch

alerts.headline: Pension enters estate April 2027
alerts.context: £680k pension will be subject to IHT from 6 April 2027. Start planning drawdown sequence now.

alerts.headline: IHT position emerging — £1.6m total estate (joint)
alerts.context: Property + pensions + ISA puts the joint estate over the combined nil-rate bands. Review.

alerts.headline: Mortgage clears in 8 years — before retirement
alerts.context: Mortgage on track to clear at age 66 — year before state pension.

aiSuggestions: How should I sequence pension and ISA drawdown for the most tax-efficient retirement income?
What is my IHT position and what can I do before 2027?
When should I start taking my pension given my target retirement age?
How much will my income change when the mortgage clears?

behaviour.risk: Transition-stage habits. You're preparing, not accumulating. Reviews have stepped up.
behaviour.action: Annual reviews become quarterly conversations as retirement approaches.
behaviour.future: Pre-retirement habits set the tone for 25+ years of decumulation ahead.

capital.risk: £680k SIPP + £240k ISA + £85k portfolio against a £1.7m target — well on course.
capital.action: Model drawdown scenarios — start date, rate, sequencing. Seven years before state pension begins.
capital.future: Being on course at 58 means decisions ahead are about optimisation, not catch-up.

tax.risk: Higher-rate taxpayer. Last window for peak pension contributions before drawdown begins.
tax.action: Max pension contributions now — relief at 40% before you drop to basic-rate in retirement.
tax.future: The final pre-retirement years often give the biggest absolute pension contributions — use them.

protection.risk: Life + CI still in place. Review whether cover amounts still match a shrinking mortgage.
protection.action: Reduce cover amounts in line with shrinking liabilities — don't pay for cover you no longer need.
protection.future: Protection needs drop as mortgage shrinks and children become independent — premiums should drop too.

cashflow.risk: £68k cash gives strong runway. Preparation fund, not emergency fund now.
cashflow.action: Start building a 24-month cash cushion for early-drawdown sequence-risk protection.
cashflow.future: Sequence-of-returns risk in the first 5 years of drawdown is the biggest threat — cash cushion defuses it.

debt.risk: £42k mortgage remaining — will be gone before pension access.
debt.action: Let the mortgage finish naturally. Pension contributions at 40% relief beat mortgage overpayment.
debt.future: Entering drawdown mortgage-free removes a fixed outflow and gives full drawdown flexibility.

estate.risk: Check pension nominations, refresh will, confirm LPA. Stage 4 is where estate planning begins.
estate.action: Book an estate-focused review — will, LPA, pension nominations reviewed in one sitting.
estate.future: Pre-drawdown estate setup avoids messy updates once drawdown is underway.

snapshots.name: Anna Finch

riskQuestionnaire.q13_note: data-inferred: state pension + drawdown + dividends + rental = 4 sources

alerts.headline: Estate approaching IHT threshold
alerts.context: Joint estate (property + SIPP + ISA) is approaching the combined NRB+RNRB. Review gifting strategy.

alerts.headline: Drawdown within guardrail
alerts.context: £22,000 annual drawdown is well within the safe withdrawal rate for your portfolio size.

aiSuggestions: Am I drawing down too much or too little given my age and portfolio?
What is my IHT position and what gifting options are available to me?
How does my state pension affect my drawdown tax position?
What happens to my SIPP if I die before my partner?

behaviour.risk: Decumulation habits — drawdown active, nominations reviewed, state pension taken.
behaviour.action: Annual review to confirm drawdown rate is sustainable across expected longevity.
behaviour.future: Steady behaviours in decumulation let capital outlast you — the purpose of all of this.

capital.risk: £520k SIPP + £310k ISA + £120k portfolio. Capital secure at £1.5m total.
capital.action: Model capital at age 90 under current drawdown — confirm safety margin is holding.
capital.future: Capital secure at 72 usually means the remaining decades are about transfer efficiency, not survival.

tax.risk: Drawdown at £22k/year. Combined with state pension this stays close to basic-rate efficiency.
tax.action: Coordinate SIPP drawdown with state pension to avoid drifting into higher-rate band.
tax.future: Keeping post-65 drawdown below the higher-rate threshold saves compounded tax over 20+ retirement years.

protection.risk: Legacy-phase protection review — do cover amounts still make sense at 72?
protection.action: Review whether legacy life cover premiums still justify the sum assured.
protection.future: At 72, some cover types lose their value — premiums can often be reduced or dropped.

cashflow.risk: £165k cash reserves strong. Reliable income from drawdown + state pension.
cashflow.action: Keep 2+ years cash cushion against sequence risk. The rest can work harder in ISA.
cashflow.future: Decumulation cashflow is about stability — reliable monthly income matters more than maximum growth.

debt.action: Stay debt-free. Avoid equity release unless genuinely needed.
debt.future: Debt-free decumulation means full capital goes to beneficiaries, not to interest payments.

estate.risk: Drawdown reducing SIPP each year. Beneficiary reviews and trust structures matter most now.
estate.action: Review pension beneficiaries and confirm nominations reflect current wishes.
estate.future: Stage 5 estate work is mostly about making the transition to beneficiaries smooth and tax-efficient.

snapshots.name: Anna Finch

alerts.headline: SIPP — post-75 death benefit review
alerts.context: Remaining £120k SIPP is taxable income for beneficiaries. Review drawdown rate and nomination.

alerts.headline: Estate well-planned — trust active
alerts.context: £80k in discretionary trust from 2020 is IHT-free. Will and LPA fully current.

aiSuggestions: How long will my current drawdown rate sustain my portfolio?
What will my children receive after IHT under the current arrangement?
Should I accelerate gifting from my estate at this stage?
How does the SIPP interact with IHT now that I am over 75?

behaviour.risk: Legacy-stage habits shift from actions to annual reviews with whoever holds your LPA.
behaviour.action: Confirm your LPA is registered and your attorney has the current pension statements.
behaviour.future: At 89 the product becomes about someone else being able to act on your behalf smoothly.

capital.risk: Capital has grown through retirement. £1.59m against a £38k target — heavily over-funded.
capital.action: Consider whether earlier gifting could reduce future IHT for your beneficiaries.
capital.future: Capital that outlasts spending becomes inheritance — the question is how much is lost to IHT vs reaches them.

tax.risk: Drawdown low, ISA substantial. Tax focus now is beneficiary tax on inheritance.
tax.action: Model ISA + SIPP + property together as an inheritance package — different tax treatments apply.
tax.future: Inherited ISAs transfer to a spouse tax-free via APS. SIPP treatment depends on age-at-death rules.

protection.risk: No cover at 89 — expected. Protection is now about documents, not policies.
protection.action: Confirm will, LPA, and digital-access instructions are current and accessible.
protection.future: At 89 the most valuable 'protection' is documents that let your family act quickly and correctly.

cashflow.risk: £88k cash gives 2+ years runway. Fixed income covers spending comfortably.
cashflow.action: Keep 2 years cash plus current-year buffer. Rest can remain invested.
cashflow.future: At 89, predictable monthly income plus cash cushion removes any reason for capital pressure.

debt.future: Debt-free through your 80s means full capital reaches beneficiaries with no interest leakage.

estate.risk: Will, LPA, beneficiaries — everything should be airtight at this stage.
estate.action: Annual review of will, LPA, and pension nominations. Confirm beneficiaries are all correctly listed.
estate.future: The work done at 89 is what your beneficiaries will be grateful for at 90 — clear, current, final.
